skill.name: managing-git
skill.description: Guides Git workflows for branches, commits, pull requests, rebasing, and release coordination.
skill.summary: Provides practical Git workflow guidance for everyday development and review tasks.
skill.author: CloudAI-X

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, network, and weak cryptography patterns, but review found a Markdown-only Git guide. The network and weak cryptography hits are false positives from ordinary wording and Markdown examples. The remaining risk is low because the guide includes real Git commands, including history-rewriting commands, that can change repository state if followed without review.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes Destructive Git Commands
low_findings.description: The skill is a Markdown guide and does not execute code by itself. It includes examples such as reset, amend, rebase, and stash commands that can rewrite history or discard local changes if a user applies them without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are explicit Git command examples in a Git workflow guide. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the file contains documentation only.

low_findings.title: Static Network And Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: No evidence found of network access, HTTP client code, cryptographic operations, or malware behavior. The network alert comes from descriptive text mentioning pull requests, and the weak cryptography alerts match Markdown or Git terminology rather than executable algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no executable network or cryptographic code in the only scanned file. The cited text is plain Markdown guidance and command examples.

content.user_title: Manage Git Workflows with Safer Branching
content.value_statement: Git workflows can become inconsistent across branches, commits, and pull requests. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for everyday version control tasks.
actual_capabilities: Explains GitHub Flow and Git Flow branching models.
Provides conventional commit formats and commit type guidance.
Suggests branch naming patterns for feature, fix, and chore work.
Supplies pull request checklists and a review-ready PR template.
Lists common Git commands for daily work, mistakes, and advanced operations.
Defines commit validation checks before pushing changes.
limitations: It provides guidance only and does not inspect repository state by itself.
It cannot verify remote branch policies, permissions, or required checks.
Some commands can rewrite history or discard local changes if used incorrectly.
Teams may need to adapt conventions to their existing release process.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Feature Branch
use_cases.description: Create a clear branch, make atomic commits, keep current with main, and prepare a pull request.

use_cases.title: Standardize Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Use consistent checklists, summaries, testing notes, and review expectations across a team.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Commit History
use_cases.description: Plan rebases, amendments, and validation steps before pushing or merging shared work.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Branch Name
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a branch name for this task: [describe task]. Use the skill conventions and explain the choice.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these changes into a conventional commit message: [summarize changes]. Include the type, scope, description, and optional body.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request summary and checklist for these changes: [describe changes]. Include testing notes and review focus.

prompt_templates.title: Plan History Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this branch situation and propose a safe cleanup plan: [describe commits, base branch, and push status]. Flag destructive steps before suggesting them.

output_examples.input: I need a branch name and commit message for adding Google login.
output_examples.output: Suggested branch: feature/AUTH-123-google-login. Suggested commit message: feat(auth): add Google login support. The branch name links the feature to the ticket and keeps the description concise.

output_examples.input: I am opening a PR for an API bug fix.
output: Summary: Fixes the API null response handling for the payment gateway.
Testing: Add the unit test result, any manual checks, and whether regression cases passed.
Review focus: Confirm error handling, user-facing response, and compatibility with existing clients.

output_examples.input: My branch has several temporary commits before review.
output_examples.output: Recommended plan: confirm the branch is not shared, save current work, review each commit, then squash or reword temporary commits. Avoid destructive cleanup on shared branches without team agreement.

best_practices: Confirm repository state before using commands that rewrite history or discard changes.
Keep commits atomic and write messages that explain the user-visible or technical purpose.
Use pull request checklists to verify tests, documentation, conflicts, and security impact.
anti_patterns: Do not apply reset, rebase, or amend commands blindly on shared branches.
Do not create large pull requests when the work can be reviewed in smaller parts.
Do not commit secrets, credentials, temporary work, or unclear messages.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and command examples. The user or agent must decide what to run.

faq.question: Which Git workflow does it recommend?
faq.answer: It recommends GitHub Flow for most projects and describes Git Flow for release-based projects.

faq.question: Can it help write conventional commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes conventional commit structure, common types, and examples for feature and fix work.

faq.question: Can it create pull request descriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides a PR checklist and template covering summary, changes, testing, and screenshots.

faq.question: Is it safe for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, if users review commands before running them. History rewrite and reset commands need extra caution.

faq.question: Does it replace team Git policies?
faq.answer: No. Teams should adapt the guidance to match branch protections, review rules, and release policies.
